title.emphasis: Section II: The Constitutional Line Between Expression and Conduct

section.para: This section explores the legal framework for analyzing conduct that may have an expressive component, a necessary step before evaluating the physical act of prostitution. The First Amendment's protections are not confined to words alone but can extend to actions that are intended to communicate a message.

title.emphasis: 2.1 Symbolic Speech and the O'Brien Test

section.para: The First Amendment's protection extends to "expressive conduct" or "symbolic speech," which includes activities like picketing, wearing armbands, and burning flags.2 However, the Supreme Court has explicitly rejected the "view that an apparently limitless variety of conduct can be labeled 'speech' whenever the person engaging in the conduct intends thereby to express an idea" (
United States v. O'Brien).4 The law therefore requires a method for distinguishing expressive action from simple, non-communicative conduct.

title.emphasis: The Spence Test for Expressive Conduct

section.para: Before a court applies any level of constitutional scrutiny to a regulation of conduct, it must first determine if the conduct is "inherently expressive".2 Under the two-part test articulated in

Spence v. Washington (1974), this requires a showing of both (1) an "intent to convey a particularized message," and (2) a great likelihood "that the message would be understood by those who viewed it".2 Conduct that is expressive only because the actor accompanies it with an explanation is not considered inherently expressive.2

If conduct is deemed expressive, government regulations that incidentally burden that expression are not automatically unconstitutional. They are analyzed under the less-stringent, four-part O'Brien test, which is a form of intermediate scrutiny.2 A government regulation is justified if it meets the following criteria:

listitem.para: It is within the constitutional power of the Government;
It furthers an important or substantial governmental interest;
The governmental interest is unrelated to the suppression of free expression; and
The incidental restriction on alleged First Amendment freedoms is no greater than is essential to the furtherance of that interest.2

section.para: The test was established in the case of David O'Brien, who burned his draft card on the steps of a courthouse to protest the Vietnam War.4 The Supreme Court upheld his conviction under a federal law prohibiting the knowing mutilation of draft cards.3 The Court applied its newly minted test and found it satisfied. It concluded that while O'Brien's act was expressive, the government had a substantial, non-speech-related interest in the smooth functioning of the Selective Service system, which required the preservation of draft cards.3 Because the law was aimed at this administrative, "noncommunicative aspect of O'Brien's conduct," it was upheld despite its incidental effect on his protest.3

title.emphasis: 2.2 The Nude Dancing Precedent—Barnes v. Glen Theatre, Inc. (1991)

section.para: The Supreme Court's decision in Barnes v. Glen Theatre, Inc. is the most proximate and instructive precedent for analyzing sexually-themed conduct under the O'Brien framework. In Barnes, the Court confronted the question of whether a state's general public indecency law could be constitutionally applied to prohibit non-obscene, totally nude dancing in adult entertainment establishments.24

A plurality of the Court, in an opinion by Chief Justice Rehnquist, conceded that nude dancing is "expressive conduct within the outer perimeters of the First Amendment, although only marginally so".20 Without engaging in a detailed
Spence analysis, the Court accepted that the erotic performance was intended to convey a message of eroticism and was likely to be understood as such by its audience.20
Despite finding the conduct expressive, the Court upheld the Indiana law requiring dancers to wear minimal coverings, such as pasties and a G-string.20 It did so by applying the four-part
O'Brien test and finding it satisfied.20

listitem.para: Substantial Government Interest: The plurality identified the state's interest in "protecting societal order and morality" as a substantial government interest sufficient to satisfy the second prong of the test.24 In a separate and highly influential concurrence, Justice Souter identified a different interest: combating the "secondary effects" of adult entertainment establishments, such as prostitution, sexual assaults, and other criminal activity.24 This "secondary effects" doctrine provides a powerful tool for the state. It allows a regulation to be justified based on potential negative societal impacts associated with the activity, thereby framing a potentially content-based moral judgment as a content-neutral public safety regulation that is more likely to survive judicial scrutiny.

Interest Unrelated to Suppression of Expression: This was the critical step in the analysis. The Court reasoned that the public indecency law was not aimed at the erotic message of the dance, but at the public nudity itself—the non-speech element of the conduct.20 The law's purpose was to ban all public nudity, regardless of whether it was combined with an expressive activity. The plurality stated that an erotic performance could be presented without state interference, so long as the performers wore a scant amount of clothing.26 This conceptual separation of the physical conduct (nudity) from the expressive message (eroticism) was key to finding the state's interest content-neutral and thus satisfying the third prong of the O'Brien test. This legal distinction allows the Court to frame the law as a general prohibition on public nudity that happens to affect an expressive activity, rather than a law targeting the expression itself, thereby avoiding the much stricter standard of review reserved for content-based regulations.
